# UPSC Prelims Ancient India: Complete Micro-Syllabus, Theme Map, and Revision Checklist
**Purpose:** a maximal-coverage markdown map for Ancient India in UPSC Prelims, expanding the broad official history line into chapters, subtopics, artefacts, sites, themes, and discussion prompts.
**Scope boundary:** from human prehistory and archaeological method to early medieval transition; ancient core is treated up to roughly the 7th/8th century CE, with art-culture and South Indian transition topics extending further where UPSC commonly overlaps them.
**Use rule:** start with chronology and sources, then proceed through material cultures, states, religions, empires, regional polities, art, science, economy, and map-based revision.
**Coverage caveat:** UPSC can frame unpredictable questions, so this file is designed as practical maximum coverage, not a promise that every possible future question is pre-listed.
**Recommended answer style for Prelims:** know chronology, evidence, location, terminology, patronage, and change-over-time rather than memorising isolated facts.
**Markdown navigation tip:** search within the file for `Chapter`, `Site`, `Artefact`, `Term`, `PYQ`, `Compare`, or the name of a dynasty/site/text.
**Source stack to consult while using this checklist:** official UPSC syllabus/notification, NCERT Themes in Indian History Part I, NIOS Ancient Indian history modules, CCRT culture material, standard Ancient India texts, ASI site notes, and previous UPSC question papers.

## Part 1 — Exact Study Order from Zero to Full Ancient India Coverage
- Study-order 01: Build a one-page timeline from prehistory to early medieval transition before reading any dynasty in detail.
- Study-order 02: Learn the difference between archaeological, literary, epigraphic, numismatic and foreign-account evidence.
- Study-order 03: Read prehistory through tool types, subsistence strategies, climate shifts, and map locations rather than as names only.
- Study-order 04: Study Neolithic and Chalcolithic cultures as regional systems, not as a single all-India stage.
- Study-order 05: Treat the Harappan civilisation as the first major UPSC anchor because it combines urbanism, craft, trade, technology and archaeology.
- Study-order 06: Move from Harappan decline to Vedic debates carefully, avoiding simplistic continuity or invasion-only explanations.
- Study-order 07: Read Rigvedic and Later Vedic separately because economy, polity, social hierarchy and ritual complexity change significantly.
- Study-order 08: Use the Iron Age, PGW, NBPW and megalithic evidence to connect material culture with state formation.
- Study-order 09: Study Mahajanapadas and Magadha using geography, agriculture, iron, trade routes, fortification, monarchy and gana-sangha comparison.
- Study-order 10: Place Buddhism, Jainism and Ajivikas inside the social world of cities, merchants, monarchies and sramana critique.
- Study-order 11: Study Mauryas through sources first: Arthashastra, Indica, Ashokan edicts, archaeology, coins and later traditions.
- Study-order 12: Analyse Ashoka separately: conquest, dhamma, edicts, pillar architecture, missionary claims, administration and limits of control.
- Study-order 13: Study post-Mauryan India as regionalisation plus intensification of trade, art schools, devotional cults and coinage.
- Study-order 14: Read Satavahanas, Kushanas, Indo-Greeks, Shakas, Parthians and Sangam polities together through networks and cultural exchange.
- Study-order 15: Study Gupta age without romantic labels: polity, land grants, Sanskritisation, sciences, coins, society, art and regional variations.
- Study-order 16: Read Vakatakas, Harsha, Pallavas and Chalukyas as transition chapters linking ancient patterns with early medieval structures.
- Study-order 17: Finally, revise through cross-cutting themes: economy, society, women, technology, labour, craft, art, religion, literature and maps.
--- <!-- divider unique 0003 -->
## Part 2 — Ancient India Chronology Spine
- P00 | Historical method and chronology | Chronology: before periodisation | Core focus: sources, evidence, dating, and interpretation.
- P01 | Lower Palaeolithic | Chronology: c. 2 million years ago onward | Core focus: Acheulian handaxe-cleaver traditions and early hominin adaptation.
- P02 | Middle Palaeolithic | Chronology: c. 150000-45000 BCE | Core focus: flake tools, prepared cores, changing ecological niches.
- P03 | Upper Palaeolithic | Chronology: c. 45000-12000 BCE | Core focus: blade tools, symbolic behaviour, broader hunter-gatherer evidence.
- P04 | Mesolithic | Chronology: c. 12000-6000 BCE | Core focus: microliths, rock art, seasonal mobility, early domestication signals.
- P05 | Early farming and Neolithic north-west | Chronology: c. 7000-3000 BCE | Core focus: Mehrgarh, settled life, domestication, ceramic and aceramic stages.
- P06 | Regional Neolithic cultures | Chronology: c. 3000-1500 BCE | Core focus: Kashmir, Belan valley, eastern India, Deccan and south Indian ash-mound traditions.
- P07 | Chalcolithic cultures | Chronology: c. 3000-700 BCE | Core focus: copper-stone cultures, regional villages, craft, pottery types and proto-urban hints.
- P08 | Early Harappan | Chronology: c. 3300-2600 BCE | Core focus: regionalisation, fortified settlements, craft specialisation, pre-urban networks.
- P09 | Mature Harappan | Chronology: c. 2600-1900 BCE | Core focus: urbanism, town planning, writing, standardisation, long-distance exchange.
- P10 | Late Harappan and post-urban phases | Chronology: c. 1900-1300 BCE | Core focus: de-urbanisation, regional transformation, settlement shifts and continuity debates.
- P11 | Rigvedic period | Chronology: c. 1500-1000 BCE | Core focus: pastoral-agrarian society, clan polity, Vedic hymns and ritual world.
- P12 | Later Vedic period | Chronology: c. 1000-600 BCE | Core focus: agriculture, iron, territoriality, varna consolidation and ritual expansion.
- P13 | Iron Age and PGW/early NBPW horizon | Chronology: c. 1200-600 BCE | Core focus: iron tools, Painted Grey Ware, northern settlements and chiefdom-to-state transition.
- P14 | Megalithic south and peninsular Iron Age | Chronology: c. 1200 BCE-300 CE | Core focus: burials, iron, ceramics, social ranking and early Tamilakam links.
- P15 | Second urbanisation and Mahajanapadas | Chronology: c. 600-300 BCE | Core focus: states, cities, coins, trade routes, republican polities and Magadhan rise.
- P16 | Religious movements of the 6th century BCE | Chronology: c. 600-300 BCE | Core focus: Buddhism, Jainism, Ajivikas, material setting and sramana traditions.
- P17 | Nanda and early Magadhan imperialism | Chronology: c. 4th century BCE | Core focus: taxation, military resources, Ganga heartland and pre-Mauryan consolidation.
- P18 | Mauryan Empire | Chronology: c. 321-185 BCE | Core focus: centralised empire, Ashokan dhamma, edicts, administration, art and imperial geography.
- P19 | Shunga-Kanva and post-Mauryan north | Chronology: c. 185-30 BCE | Core focus: regional courts, Brahmanical forms, Buddhist monument patronage and political fragmentation.
- P20 | Indo-Greek, Shaka and Parthian phases | Chronology: c. 2nd century BCE-1st century CE | Core focus: north-western contacts, coins, Hellenistic motifs and frontier polities.
- P21 | Kushana age | Chronology: c. 1st-3rd century CE | Core focus: Central Asian connections, Kanishka, Mahayana, trade routes and Gandhara-Mathura art.
- P22 | Satavahana and western Deccan | Chronology: c. 1st century BCE-3rd century CE | Core focus: Deccan state formation, cave donations, Prakrit inscriptions and Roman trade.
- P23 | Sangam age and early Tamilakam | Chronology: c. 300 BCE-300 CE | Core focus: Chera-Chola-Pandya polities, tinai ecology, ports, hero stones and classical Tamil literature.
- P24 | Early Gupta and classical age | Chronology: c. 300-550 CE | Core focus: Sanskrit culture, polity, coins, temple beginnings, literature and sciences.
- P25 | Vakataka and post-Gupta transition | Chronology: c. 250-600 CE | Core focus: Deccan-north links, Ajanta, land grants and regional court cultures.
- P26 | Harsha, Maukharis and north Indian transition | Chronology: c. 550-750 CE | Core focus: regional kingship, Chinese accounts, religious patronage and post-Gupta politics.
- P27 | Pallava-Chalukya and early medieval transition | Chronology: c. 550-850 CE | Core focus: stone temples, Sanskrit-regional synthesis, land grants and peninsular state formation.
- P28 | Early Bhakti and temple-centred culture | Chronology: c. 6th-9th century CE | Core focus: Alvar-Nayanar devotion, Agamic worship, temple institutions and regional language growth.
--- <!-- divider unique 0004 -->
## Part 3 — Official-Style Expansion of the Broad UPSC History Line
- UPSC-broad-line 01: Ancient India sits within “History of India” for Prelims GS Paper I and also overlaps with Indian culture, art, architecture, religion, literature, science and society.
- UPSC-broad-line 02: Prelims does not provide a micro-syllabus for Ancient India, so coverage must be constructed from recurring PYQ themes, NCERT/NIOS coverage, standard history texts and culture sources.
- UPSC-broad-line 03: Ancient India questions frequently test evidence-based discrimination: which site, which period, which source, which inscription, which ruler, which school of art, and which material object.
- UPSC-broad-line 04: The safest sequence is chronology first, then sources, then region-wise cultures, then dynasties, then cross-cutting themes.
- UPSC-broad-line 05: Every topic should be revised in three modes: timeline mode, map mode and source-evidence mode.
--- <!-- divider unique 0005 -->
## Part 4 — Core Chapter Names, Subtopics and Discussion Points
### M01 Sources, Method and Historiography
#### CH001 — Meaning of ancient India and periodisation
- CH001 overview: Study Meaning of ancient India and periodisation as part of M01 Sources, Method and Historiography, linking facts with chronology, source evidence, map location and comparison logic.
- CH001 microtopic 01: prehistoric, protohistoric and historic distinctions.
- CH001 microtopic 02: ancient versus early medieval boundary debates.
- CH001 microtopic 03: BCE/CE chronology conventions.
- CH001 microtopic 04: relative chronology and absolute chronology.
- CH001 microtopic 05: culture phase versus political dynasty.
- CH001 microtopic 06: regional variation in historical stages.
- CH001 microtopic 07: limits of textbook period labels.
- CH001 microtopic 08: UPSC tendency to mix cultural and political chronology.
- CH001 microtopic 09: timeline anchors from Mehrgarh to Harsha.
- CH001 microtopic 10: change-over-time revision method.
- CH001 discussion point — Definition: Define the topic precisely and separate it from adjacent phases or dynasties. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Chronology: Place it before and after the correct period, ruler, culture or text. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Source base: Identify whether the evidence is archaeological, literary, inscriptional, numismatic or foreign-account based. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Map anchor: Locate the principal region, river, route, port, cave, capital or cultural zone. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Economy: Connect production, surplus, craft, trade, coins, guilds or agrarian expansion to the topic. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Society: Note class, caste, clan, gender, labour, household, donor or occupational patterns. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Polity: Check whether authority is tribal, monarchical, republican, imperial, regional or temple-centred. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Religion: Trace ritual, sect, doctrine, patronage, iconography or pilgrimage significance. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Technology: Connect tools, metallurgy, ceramics, irrigation, textiles, writing or transport to the topic. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Art-culture: Check monuments, sculptures, paintings, literature, language, scripts or performance traditions. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — Continuity: Ask what continues from earlier phases and what changes in later phases. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.
- CH001 discussion point — UPSC trap: Avoid overgeneralisation, anachronism, single-cause explanation and site-feature confusion. Applied focus: Meaning of ancient India and periodisation.

- CH106 discussion point — Source base: Identify whether the evidence is archaeological, literary, inscriptional, numismatic or foreign-account based. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Map anchor: Locate the principal region, river, route, port, cave, capital or cultural zone. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Economy: Connect production, surplus, craft, trade, coins, guilds or agrarian expansion to the topic. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Society: Note class, caste, clan, gender, labour, household, donor or occupational patterns. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Polity: Check whether authority is tribal, monarchical, republican, imperial, regional or temple-centred. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Religion: Trace ritual, sect, doctrine, patronage, iconography or pilgrimage significance. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Technology: Connect tools, metallurgy, ceramics, irrigation, textiles, writing or transport to the topic. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Art-culture: Check monuments, sculptures, paintings, literature, language, scripts or performance traditions. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — Continuity: Ask what continues from earlier phases and what changes in later phases. Applied focus: Environmental history.
- CH106 discussion point — UPSC trap: Avoid overgeneralisation, anachronism, single-cause explanation and site-feature confusion. Applied focus: Environmental history.
--- <!-- divider unique 0006 -->

## Part 6 — Source-Evidence Discipline Checklist
### Evidence category — Archaeology
- Evidence drill Archaeology-what it can prove: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask what it can prove before accepting a fact.
- Evidence drill Archaeology-what it cannot prove: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask what it cannot prove before accepting a fact.
- Evidence drill Archaeology-chronology clue: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask chronology clue before accepting a fact.
- Evidence drill Archaeology-map clue: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask map clue before accepting a fact.
- Evidence drill Archaeology-UPSC misuse risk: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask UPSC misuse risk before accepting a fact.

- Evidence drill Archaeology-terminology to remember: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask terminology to remember before accepting a fact.
- Evidence drill Archaeology-link with economy: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask link with economy before accepting a fact.

- Evidence drill Archaeology-link with polity: For Ancient India, Archaeology means material remains, sites, artefacts, tools, ceramics, burials and settlement layouts; while revising, ask link with polity before accepting a fact.

## Part 7 — Site Gazetteer for Map-Based Prelims Revision
### SITE001 — Bori
- SITE001 location marker: Bori is associated with Maharashtra; mark it on an Ancient India outline map.
- SITE001 primary phase: Revise Bori under Lower Palaeolithic, and avoid placing it in a wrong chronological block.
- SITE001 key feature: Remember early stone-age sequence and volcanic ash context as the highest-yield identifier.
- SITE001 evidence type: Ask whether Bori is mainly known through excavation, inscription, monument, coin, text or trade artefact.
- SITE001 comparison hook: Compare Bori with another site from the same phase to avoid one-site memorisation.
- SITE001 UPSC trap: Do not treat every feature of Bori as applicable to all of Ancient India.
- SITE001 map drill: Link Bori with its nearest river, coast, pass, plateau, trade route or political region.
- SITE001 revision output: Prepare one MCQ statement that correctly distinguishes Bori from two similar sites.

## Part 8 — Glossary of High-Yield Ancient India Terms
### TERM001 — Aceramic Neolithic
- TERM001 definition: Neolithic phase before regular pottery use.
- TERM001 prelims use: connect Aceramic Neolithic with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM001 trap control: avoid using Aceramic Neolithic outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM002 — Acheulian
- TERM002 definition: Lower Palaeolithic handaxe-cleaver tradition.
- TERM002 prelims use: connect Acheulian with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM002 trap control: avoid using Acheulian outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM003 — Agama
- TERM003 definition: canonical or sectarian religious textual tradition, especially Jain and later temple traditions.
- TERM003 prelims use: connect Agama with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM003 trap control: avoid using Agama outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM004 — Agrahara
- TERM004 definition: land grant settlement often associated with Brahmanical donees.
- TERM004 prelims use: connect Agrahara with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM004 trap control: avoid using Agrahara outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM005 — Ahimsa
- TERM005 definition: non-violence central to Jainism and important in Buddhism.
- TERM005 prelims use: connect Ahimsa with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM005 trap control: avoid using Ahimsa outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM006 — Ajivika
- TERM006 definition: sramana sect associated with Makkhali Gosala and niyati doctrine.
- TERM006 prelims use: connect Ajivika with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM006 trap control: avoid using Ajivika outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM007 — Akshapataladhikrita
- TERM007 definition: record-keeping officer term in administrative contexts.
- TERM007 prelims use: connect Akshapataladhikrita with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM007 trap control: avoid using Akshapataladhikrita outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM008 — Alvar
- TERM008 definition: Tamil Vaishnava devotional poet-saint.
- TERM008 prelims use: connect Alvar with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM008 trap control: avoid using Alvar outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM009 — Amatya
- TERM009 definition: minister or official in ancient polity terminology.
- TERM009 prelims use: connect Amatya with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM009 trap control: avoid using Amatya outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM010 — Anatman
- TERM010 definition: Buddhist doctrine denying a permanent self.
- TERM010 prelims use: connect Anatman with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM010 trap control: avoid using Anatman outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM011 — Anekantavada
- TERM011 definition: Jain doctrine of many-sided reality.
- TERM011 prelims use: connect Anekantavada with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM011 trap control: avoid using Anekantavada outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM012 — Anguttara Nikaya
- TERM012 definition: Buddhist text listing Mahajanapadas among other material.
- TERM012 prelims use: connect Anguttara Nikaya with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM012 trap control: avoid using Anguttara Nikaya outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM013 — Antyaja
- TERM013 definition: socially marginal groups in later Brahmanical texts.
- TERM013 prelims use: connect Antyaja with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM013 trap control: avoid using Antyaja outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM014 — Aranyakas
- TERM014 definition: forest texts linked with Vedic ritual speculation.
- TERM014 prelims use: connect Aranyakas with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM014 trap control: avoid using Aranyakas outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM015 — Ardhamagadhi
- TERM015 definition: Prakrit associated with Jain texts.
- TERM015 prelims use: connect Ardhamagadhi with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM015 trap control: avoid using Ardhamagadhi outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM016 — Arthashastra
- TERM016 definition: statecraft treatise associated with Kautilya/Chanakya tradition.
- TERM016 prelims use: connect Arthashastra with its period, source, region and doctrinal or institutional context.
- TERM016 trap control: avoid using Arthashastra outside its chronological or textual setting unless continuity is explicitly established.
### TERM017 — Aryavarta
- TERM017 definition: northern sacred-cultural region in Brahmanical texts.

--- <!-- divider unique 0010 -->
## Part 9 — Artefact, Technology and Material-Culture Index
### ART001 — handaxe
- ART001 identification: Lower Palaeolithic stone tool used as a broad Acheulian marker.
- ART001 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does handaxe indicate?
- ART001 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used handaxe?
- ART001 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from handaxe without checking its site distribution and dating.
### ART002 — cleaver
- ART002 identification: Lower Palaeolithic heavy-duty tool often paired with handaxe traditions.
- ART002 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does cleaver indicate?
- ART002 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used cleaver?
- ART002 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from cleaver without checking its site distribution and dating.
### ART003 — flake tool
- ART003 identification: stone flake used widely in Middle Palaeolithic contexts.
- ART003 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does flake tool indicate?
- ART003 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used flake tool?
- ART003 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from flake tool without checking its site distribution and dating.
### ART004 — scraper
- ART004 identification: tool for scraping hides, wood or other materials.
- ART004 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does scraper indicate?
- ART004 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used scraper?
- ART004 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from scraper without checking its site distribution and dating.
### ART005 — burin
- ART005 identification: Upper Palaeolithic engraving or cutting tool.
- ART005 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does burin indicate?
- ART005 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used burin?
- ART005 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from burin without checking its site distribution and dating.
### ART006 — microlith
- ART006 identification: small stone blade used in composite tools, high-yield Mesolithic marker.
- ART006 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does microlith indicate?
- ART006 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used microlith?
- ART006 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from microlith without checking its site distribution and dating.
### ART007 — quern
- ART007 identification: grinding stone linked with plant processing.
- ART007 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does quern indicate?
- ART007 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used quern?
- ART007 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from quern without checking its site distribution and dating.
### ART008 — ground stone axe
- ART008 identification: Neolithic tool linked with clearing, woodworking and settled life.
- ART008 evidence question: Which period, site, material, technique and social use does ground stone axe indicate?
- ART008 labour question: What kind of worker, workshop, household or patronage structure could have produced or used ground stone axe?
- ART008 UPSC trap: Do not infer a whole civilisation from ground stone axe without checking its site distribution and dating.

## Part 10 — Dynasties, Rulers and Texts Checklist
### POL001 — Haryanka dynasty
- POL001 rulers: Bimbisara and Ajatashatru.
- POL001 focus: Magadha rise through diplomacy, conquest and strategic capitals.
- POL001 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL001 comparison: compare Haryanka dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL002 — Shishunaga dynasty
- POL002 rulers: Shishunaga and Kalashoka traditions.
- POL002 focus: Magadhan succession and pre-Nanda consolidation.
- POL002 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL002 comparison: compare Shishunaga dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL003 — Nanda dynasty
- POL003 rulers: Mahapadma Nanda and Dhana Nanda traditions.
- POL003 focus: large resources, taxation and pre-Mauryan imperial scale.
- POL003 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL003 comparison: compare Nanda dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL004 — Maurya dynasty
- POL004 rulers: Chandragupta, Bindusara, Ashoka.
- POL004 focus: first major subcontinental empire and edictal state communication.
- POL004 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL004 comparison: compare Maurya dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL005 — Shunga dynasty
- POL005 rulers: Pushyamitra and successors.
- POL005 focus: post-Mauryan regional north Indian power.
- POL005 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL005 comparison: compare Shunga dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL006 — Kanva dynasty
- POL006 rulers: Vasudeva Kanva and successors.
- POL006 focus: short post-Shunga line in north Indian chronology.
- POL006 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL006 comparison: compare Kanva dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL007 — Indo-Greek kingdoms
- POL007 rulers: Demetrius, Menander and others.
- POL007 focus: north-western coinage and Hellenistic contact.
- POL007 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL007 comparison: compare Indo-Greek kingdoms with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL008 — Western Kshatrapas
- POL008 rulers: Rudradaman I.
- POL008 focus: Sanskrit inscription, western India and Satavahana interaction.
- POL008 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL008 comparison: compare Western Kshatrapas with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL009 — Kushana dynasty
- POL009 rulers: Kujula Kadphises, Vima Kadphises, Kanishka.
- POL009 focus: Central Asian links, coinage, art and Mahayana patronage.
- POL009 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL009 comparison: compare Kushana dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL010 — Satavahana dynasty
- POL010 rulers: Gautamiputra Satakarni and Pulumavi.
- POL010 focus: Deccan state, Prakrit inscriptions and trade routes.
- POL010 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL010 comparison: compare Satavahana dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL011 — Chera polity
- POL011 rulers: Sangam Cheras.
- POL011 focus: west-coast trade, pepper and Tamilakam ecology.
- POL011 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL011 comparison: compare Chera polity with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL012 — Chola polity
- POL012 rulers: early Cholas.
- POL012 focus: Kaveri region, Puhar and Sangam literary memory.
- POL012 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL012 comparison: compare Chola polity with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL013 — Pandya polity
- POL013 rulers: early Pandyas.
- POL013 focus: Madurai, pearls and southern Tamilakam.
- POL013 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL013 comparison: compare Pandya polity with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL014 — Gupta dynasty
- POL014 rulers: Chandragupta I to Skandagupta.
- POL014 focus: classical Sanskrit polity, coins, art and administration.
- POL014 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL014 comparison: compare Gupta dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL015 — Vakataka dynasty
- POL015 rulers: Pravarasena, Prabhavati Gupta, Harishena.
- POL015 focus: Deccan-Gupta link and Ajanta patronage.
- POL015 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL015 comparison: compare Vakataka dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL016 — Pushyabhuti dynasty
- POL016 rulers: Harshavardhana.
- POL016 focus: north Indian post-Gupta regional empire.
- POL016 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL016 comparison: compare Pushyabhuti dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL017 — Pallava dynasty
- POL017 rulers: Mahendravarman and Narasimhavarman.
- POL017 focus: Kanchi, Mahabalipuram and early stone temples.
- POL017 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL017 comparison: compare Pallava dynasty with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### POL018 — Chalukya dynasty of Badami
- POL018 rulers: Pulakeshin II.
- POL018 focus: Deccan power, Aihole inscription and temple experiments.
- POL018 revise: capital/core region, inscriptions, coins, religion, art and rival polities.
- POL018 comparison: compare Chalukya dynasty of Badami with the polity immediately before and after it in chronology.
### TEXT001 — Rigveda
- TEXT001 category: Early Vedic hymns.
- TEXT001 focus: pastoral society, early polity and ritual worldview.
- TEXT001 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Rigveda as direct evidence.
- TEXT001 prelims drill: match Rigveda with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT002 — Atharvaveda
- TEXT002 category: Vedic text.
- TEXT002 focus: ritual, magic, social concerns and later Vedic context.
- TEXT002 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Atharvaveda as direct evidence.
- TEXT002 prelims drill: match Atharvaveda with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT003 — Brahmanas
- TEXT003 category: Vedic prose texts.
- TEXT003 focus: sacrificial interpretation and ritual complexity.
- TEXT003 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Brahmanas as direct evidence.
- TEXT003 prelims drill: match Brahmanas with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT004 — Upanishads
- TEXT004 category: philosophical Vedic texts.
- TEXT004 focus: atman, brahman, karma and renunciation ideas.
- TEXT004 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Upanishads as direct evidence.
- TEXT004 prelims drill: match Upanishads with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT005 — Panini Ashtadhyayi
- TEXT005 category: grammar treatise.
- TEXT005 focus: Sanskrit grammar and north-western intellectual context.
- TEXT005 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Panini Ashtadhyayi as direct evidence.
- TEXT005 prelims drill: match Panini Ashtadhyayi with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT006 — Arthashastra
- TEXT006 category: statecraft treatise.
- TEXT006 focus: administration, economy, espionage and interstate theory.
- TEXT006 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Arthashastra as direct evidence.
- TEXT006 prelims drill: match Arthashastra with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT007 — Indica
- TEXT007 category: Greek account by Megasthenes tradition.
- TEXT007 focus: Mauryan society and administration with caution.
- TEXT007 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Indica as direct evidence.
- TEXT007 prelims drill: match Indica with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT008 — Tripitaka
- TEXT008 category: Buddhist canon.
- TEXT008 focus: doctrine, monastic discipline and early Buddhist community.
- TEXT008 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Tripitaka as direct evidence.
- TEXT008 prelims drill: match Tripitaka with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT009 — Jatakas
- TEXT009 category: Buddhist stories.
- TEXT009 focus: social and economic clues of early historic world.
- TEXT009 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Jatakas as direct evidence.
- TEXT009 prelims drill: match Jatakas with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT010 — Jain Agamas
- TEXT010 category: Jain canonical texts.
- TEXT010 focus: Jain doctrine and monastic tradition.
- TEXT010 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Jain Agamas as direct evidence.
- TEXT010 prelims drill: match Jain Agamas with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT011 — Kalpa Sutra
- TEXT011 category: Jain text.
- TEXT011 focus: Tirthankara traditions and sectarian memory.
- TEXT011 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Kalpa Sutra as direct evidence.
- TEXT011 prelims drill: match Kalpa Sutra with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT012 — Milindapanha
- TEXT012 category: Buddhist text.
- TEXT012 focus: dialogue of Menander/Milinda and Nagasena.
- TEXT012 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Milindapanha as direct evidence.
- TEXT012 prelims drill: match Milindapanha with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT013 — Manusmriti
- TEXT013 category: Brahmanical normative text.
- TEXT013 focus: varna, gender, law and social ideals with caution.
- TEXT013 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Manusmriti as direct evidence.
- TEXT013 prelims drill: match Manusmriti with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT014 — Mahabharata
- TEXT014 category: epic.
- TEXT014 focus: layered text with polity, ethics, kinship and dharma themes.
- TEXT014 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Mahabharata as direct evidence.
- TEXT014 prelims drill: match Mahabharata with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT015 — Ramayana
- TEXT015 category: epic.
- TEXT015 focus: layered text with kingship, dharma and cultural geography.
- TEXT015 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Ramayana as direct evidence.
- TEXT015 prelims drill: match Ramayana with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT016 — Sangam corpus
- TEXT016 category: early Tamil literature.
- TEXT016 focus: tinai ecology, gift, war, love, ports and polities.
- TEXT016 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Sangam corpus as direct evidence.
- TEXT016 prelims drill: match Sangam corpus with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT017 — Silappadikaram
- TEXT017 category: Tamil epic.
- TEXT017 focus: post-Sangam cultural world and urban-commercial motifs.
- TEXT017 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Silappadikaram as direct evidence.
- TEXT017 prelims drill: match Silappadikaram with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT018 — Periplus of the Erythraean Sea
- TEXT018 category: Greco-Roman trade text.
- TEXT018 focus: ports, goods and Indian Ocean trade.
- TEXT018 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Periplus of the Erythraean Sea as direct evidence.
- TEXT018 prelims drill: match Periplus of the Erythraean Sea with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT019 — Allahabad/Prayag Prashasti
- TEXT019 category: Gupta inscription.
- TEXT019 focus: Samudragupta conquests and political ideology.
- TEXT019 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Allahabad/Prayag Prashasti as direct evidence.
- TEXT019 prelims drill: match Allahabad/Prayag Prashasti with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.
### TEXT020 — Aihole inscription
- TEXT020 category: Chalukya inscription.
- TEXT020 focus: Pulakeshin II, Ravikirti and Deccan political claims.
- TEXT020 source caution: date, authorship, interpolation and normative bias must be checked before using Aihole inscription as direct evidence.
- TEXT020 prelims drill: match Aihole inscription with period, language, religious affiliation and one historical use.

## Part 16 — 100-Day Ancient India Completion Plan

--- <!-- divider unique 0020 -->
## Part 19 — How to Convert This File into Notes
- Notes method 01: Convert every chapter into a one-page note with chronology, source, map, theme and trap columns.
- Notes method 02: Convert every site into a flashcard with front side as site name and back side as period, state/country, feature and confusion pair.
- Notes method 03: Convert every artefact into a flashcard with front side as object name and back side as period, material, labour, site and inference limit.
- Notes method 04: Convert every term into a flashcard with front side as term and back side as definition, period, source and false-friend term.
- Notes method 05: Convert comparison prompts into two-column tables and revise them during the final month.
- Notes method 06: Convert PYQ drills into true/false statements and practice elimination rather than memorising paragraphs.
- Notes method 07: Keep a separate error notebook for wrong chronology, wrong map, wrong source, wrong dynasty and wrong art-school mistakes.
- Notes method 08: For every topic, ask whether UPSC could frame it as a pair, a sequence, a map, a source, a term-definition or an art-culture identification question.
--- <!-- divider unique 0021 -->
--- <!-- divider unique 0022 -->

## Part 22 — Chapter-wise MCQ Stem Bank
- MCQ stem 00001: Which of the following statements is correct about Meaning of ancient India and periodisation; frame options using chronology, source, map and one deceptive near-match.

## Part 25 — Polity and Text Cross-Theme Extension

## Part 26 — Integrated Three-Fact Drills
- Integrated drill 0001: Link Meaning of ancient India and periodisation + Bori + Aceramic Neolithic + handaxe; produce three facts and one false option using `early stone-age sequence and volcanic ash context`, `Neolithic phase before regular pottery use`, and `Lower Palaeolithic stone tool used as a broad Acheulian marker`.
- Integrated drill 0002: Link Archaeological sources and excavation logic + Attirampakkam + Acheulian + cleaver; produce three facts and one false option using `Acheulian chronology and transition evidence`, `Lower Palaeolithic handaxe-cleaver tradition`, and `Lower Palaeolithic heavy-duty tool often paired with handaxe traditions`.
- Integrated drill 0003: Link Literary sources of ancient India + Hunsgi + Agama + flake tool; produce three facts and one false option using `tool-rich valley landscape`, `canonical or sectarian religious textual tradition, especially Jain and later temple traditions`, and `stone flake used widely in Middle Palaeolithic contexts`.
- Integrated drill 0004: Link Epigraphy as historical evidence + Isampur + Agrahara + scraper; produce three facts and one false option using `quarry-cum-workshop evidence`, `land grant settlement often associated with Brahmanical donees`, and `tool for scraping hides, wood or other materials`.
- Integrated drill 0005: Link Numismatics for Prelims + Bhimbetka + Ahimsa + burin; produce three facts and one false option using `rock shelters and paintings`, `non-violence central to Jainism and important in Buddhism`, and `Upper Palaeolithic engraving or cutting tool`.
- Integrated drill 0006: Link Foreign accounts and travel narratives + Adamgarh + Ajivika + microlith; produce three facts and one false option using `rock shelters and faunal hints`, `sramana sect associated with Makkhali Gosala and niyati doctrine`, and `small stone blade used in composite tools, high-yield Mesolithic marker`.
- Integrated drill 0007: Link Historical geography of ancient India + Bagor + Akshapataladhikrita + quern; produce three facts and one false option using `microliths and pastoral-domestication debate`, `record-keeping officer term in administrative contexts`, and `grinding stone linked with plant processing`.
- Integrated drill 0008: Link Historiographical debates + Langhnaj + Alvar + ground stone axe; produce three facts and one false option using `hunter-gatherer burials and microliths`, `Tamil Vaishnava devotional poet-saint`, and `Neolithic tool linked with clearing, woodworking and settled life`.
- Integrated drill 0009: Link Lower Palaeolithic cultures + Sarai Nahar Rai + Amatya + bone tool; produce three facts and one false option using `burials and Ganga plain hunter-gatherers`, `minister or official in ancient polity terminology`, and `tool category especially notable in Kashmir Neolithic and Chirand contexts`.
- Integrated drill 0010: Link Middle Palaeolithic cultures + Mahadaha + Anatman + ash mound; produce three facts and one false option using `burials and subsistence evidence`, `Buddhist doctrine denying a permanent self`, and `south Indian Neolithic feature linked with cattle pastoralism and ritual hypotheses`.
- Integrated drill 0011: Link Upper Palaeolithic cultures + Chopani Mando + Anekantavada + black-and-red ware; produce three facts and one false option using `transition from foraging to early food production`, `Jain doctrine of many-sided reality`, and `ceramic type appearing in several Chalcolithic and Iron Age contexts`.
- Integrated drill 0012: Link Mesolithic cultures + Kurnool caves + Anguttara Nikaya + OCP pottery; produce three facts and one false option using `cave evidence and bone-tool associations`, `Buddhist text listing Mahajanapadas among other material`, and `Ochre Coloured Pottery linked with upper doab and copper hoards`.
- Integrated drill 0013: Link Prehistoric rock art + Mehrgarh + Antyaja + PGW pottery; produce three facts and one false option using `early farming, domestication and pre-Harappan sequence`, `socially marginal groups in later Brahmanical texts`, and `Painted Grey Ware linked with Iron Age upper Ganga-Yamuna region`.
- Integrated drill 0014: Link Hunter-gatherer subsistence + Kili Ghul Mohammad + Aranyakas + NBPW pottery; produce three facts and one false option using `early village context`, `forest texts linked with Vedic ritual speculation`, and `Northern Black Polished Ware linked with second urbanisation`.
- Integrated drill 0015: Link Mehrgarh and early farming + Burzahom + Ardhamagadhi + rouletted ware; produce three facts and one false option using `pit dwellings and bone tools`, `Prakrit associated with Jain texts`, and `ceramic associated with Indo-Roman trade contexts`.
- Integrated drill 0016: Link Kashmir Neolithic + Gufkral + Arthashastra + Harappan baked brick; produce three facts and one false option using `pit dwellings and Neolithic levels`, `statecraft treatise associated with Kautilya/Chanakya tradition`, and `standardised brick material showing urban planning and craft organisation`.
- Integrated drill 0017: Link Belan valley and middle Ganga Neolithic + Koldihwa + Aryavarta + Harappan seal; produce three facts and one false option using `rice evidence debate and ground stone tools`, `northern sacred-cultural region in Brahmanical texts`, and `steatite object with animal motif and script used in exchange or identity marking`.
- Integrated drill 0018: Link Eastern and north-eastern Neolithic + Mahagara + Ashramas + sealings; produce three facts and one false option using `cattle pen and early farming evidence`, `four life-stage concept in Brahmanical thought`, and `impressions of seals on clay used in administrative or exchange contexts`.
- Integrated drill 0019: Link South Indian Neolithic and ash mounds + Lahuradewa + Ashvamedha + standardised weight; produce three facts and one false option using `early rice-related evidence`, `horse sacrifice associated with royal sovereignty`, and `Harappan cubical weight system implying regulated exchange`.
- Integrated drill 0020: Link Ahar-Banas culture + Chirand + Atman + spindle whorl; produce three facts and one false option using `bone tools and riverine settlement`, `self or soul concept in Upanishadic and later traditions`, and `spinning implement used to infer textile production`.
- Integrated drill 0021: Link Malwa and Kayatha cultures + Daojali Hading + Aupanishadic + loom-related implement; produce three facts and one false option using `cord-impressed pottery and ground tools`, `related to the Upanishadic philosophical corpus`, and `textile-production clue where archaeologically attested`.
- Integrated drill 0022: Link Jorwe culture and Deccan Chalcolithic + Sanganakallu + Ayagapata + carnelian bead; produce three facts and one false option using `ash-mound complex and tool production`, `Jain votive tablet especially associated with Mathura`, and `high-value bead often associated with Harappan and early historic craft`.
- Integrated drill 0023: Link Copper hoards and Ochre Coloured Pottery + Kupgal + Ayuktaka + etched carnelian bead; produce three facts and one false option using `ash mounds and rock bruising`, `administrative functionary in Gupta or earlier contexts`, and `specialised ornament showing advanced bead technology`.
- Integrated drill 0024: Link Painted Grey Ware horizon + Piklihal + Bali + faience object; produce three facts and one false option using `pastoral camp evidence`, `offering or tribute in Vedic and later contexts`, and `silica-based glazed material valued for technical difficulty`.
- Integrated drill 0025: Link Early Harappan regional cultures + Utnur + Bhaga + shell bangle; produce three facts and one false option using `ash mound evidence`, `share or tax-like extraction from produce`, and `ornament and craft product linked with coastal procurement`.
- Integrated drill 0026: Link Mature Harappan urban planning + Tekkalakota + Bhagavata + lapis lazuli; produce three facts and one false option using `settlement and tool evidence`, `early Vaishnava devotional tradition`, and `blue stone imported from Afghanistan region networks`.
- Integrated drill 0027: Link Harappan agriculture and food + Hallur + Bhikshu + chert blade; produce three facts and one false option using `early iron and south Indian sequence`, `Buddhist monk`, and `Harappan cutting tool often linked with specialised production`.
